repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

## Problem
On Windows, path separators may become inconsistent for various reasons, which makes normalization quite painful(e.g neovim#32590, neovim#28384, neovim#25033, neovim#23639), and makes some issues harder to address(e.g. neovim#8587).

## Solution
Solution:
Normalize paths to `/` at the entry boundaries and always use it internally, converting back only in rare cases where `\` is really needed (e.g. cmd.exe/bat scripts?).

This is the first PR for #36112, mainly ensuring that separators in options and buf names are `/`.

Note:
* some funcs won't respect shellslash. e.g. `expand/fnamemodify`
* some funcs still respect shellslash, but will be updated in a follow PR. e.g. `ex_pwd/f_chdir/f_exepath/f_getcwd`
* uv's built-in funcs always return `\`. e.g. `uv.cwd/uv.exepath`

comment_data.body: Not sure if there are some programs that also require backslashes when the path contains separators—this might be a case for `shellslash`?

The program name format only matters to win32 (well, there could be some edge case where a program inspects its own arg0). I don't think we need to adjust slashes here.

But if we do need to adjust, in all cases we should always use a util function such as `slash_adjust` so that we can easily find all cases.

The logic here might not be very polished, and I haven't fully decided where it should live. For now, `slash_adjust` still respects `shellslash`, but `cmd.exe` requires backslashes when the path contains separators—otherwise it may fail with unpredictable errors (e.g. `The syntax of the command is incorrect`). My earlier thought was that if we always normalize to `\` here, we could align with pwsh's behavior, where `cmd` can still be invoked correctly even if the path contains `/`.

If we plan to have `fnamemodify()` return `\` slashes then generally it is useful for tests to reflect that. I.e. in this case keep the old code which asserted `\` (`pathsep`) slashes.

Alternatively, if `fnamemodify()` always returns `/` slashes then we shouldn't need `t.fix_slashes`?

For now, it probably makes sense for `fnamemodify()` to always return `/` slashes.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -97,7 +97,7 @@ describe('vim.secure', function()
       screen:expect([[
         ^let g:foobar = 42{MATCH: +}|
         {1:~{MATCH: +}}|*2
-        {2:]] .. fn.fnamemodify(cwd, ':~') .. pathsep .. [[Xfile [RO]{MATCH: +}}|
+        {2:]] .. t.fix_slashes(fn.fnamemodify(cwd, ':~')) .. [[/Xfile [RO]{MATCH: +}}|

-          .. state_dir
-          .. ''

-          .. 'view'
-          .. (path_sep):rep(2),
+        ',=,=,' .. path_sep .. '' .. state_dir .. '' .. path_sep .. 'view' .. (path_sep):rep(2),

comment_data.body: This is a good case to note: libuv (`vim.uv`) will presumably continue to return `\` slashes on Windows. We should note that in docs.

-    local sep = get_pathsep()
+    local wd = t.fix_slashes(vim.uv.cwd())

comment_data.body: don't need fix_slashes if `sep='/'`
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -326,8 +326,8 @@ describe(':source', function()
 end)
 
 it('$HOME is not shortened in filepath in v:stacktrace from sourced file', function()
-  local sep = n.get_pathsep()
-  local xhome = table.concat({ vim.uv.cwd(), 'Xhome' }, sep)
+  local sep = '/'
+  local xhome = t.fix_slashes(table.concat({ vim.uv.cwd(), 'Xhome' }, sep))

comment_data.body: Ah, I guess the old code may use `fnamemodify` mainly for convenience in local test. :D That code actually represents a buf name. In Windows CI, the `cwd` is `D:\a\neovim\neovim`, `:~` doesn't change separators, this is the behavior on master, so I added `fix_slashes` here. Also, since this PR was already 40+ files, I planned to handle `expand/fnamemodify/chdir/pwd...` in the second PR. That would require updating more tests and may remove many `fix_slashes`.

     call assert_equal([], (glob('abc[glob]def\*', 0, 1)))

      t.ok(nil, (path:find('/') and not path:find('\\')))

comment_data.body: should the opt_idx check be outside of the for loop?
why not use `forward_slash`, or `strchrsub` ?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1516,6 +1516,38 @@ void slash_adjust(char *p)
 }
 #endif
 
+/// Primary purpose is path separator conversion.
+static char *path_replace_char(char *p, char a, char b)

comment_data.body: not sure if we need this. can keep it for now, but let's revisit this.
why are `\` slashes needed here?

this could be a problem, though it existed before this pr. just for reference:

> `leader` is a direct pointer to `compl_leader.data` or compl_orig_text.data (module-global String variables).  ... If a user is completing a path like foo\bar on Windows, this call silently rewrites the leader to foo/bar in the global state. That mutated leader then flows into:
> - ins_compl_leader() calls elsewhere
> - Comparisons in ins_compl_match_flush, ins_compl_fixcol, etc.
> - The text ultimately inserted into the buffer

Nope, this is to keep it consistent with the original test intent: [do not use `completeslash` for `expand('~')`](https://github.com/vim/vim/pull/7021).

After this PR, `expand('~')` will always return forward slash, so we need to update the assertion to:
`call assert_false(expand('~') =~ '\\')` 
If we don't flip this option, it ends up looking like `completeslash=slash` affects the result of `expand()`.

comment_data.body: `\`? , `TO_SLASH` normalizes `\` into `/`. 😂
changed to `strchrsub`, `forward_slash` will be removed in the follow PR.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1516,6 +1516,38 @@ void slash_adjust(char *p)
 }
 #endif
 
+/// Primary purpose is path separator conversion.
+static char *path_replace_char(char *p, char a, char b)

comment_data.body: Has removed it.  It was just used in a few places in early commits. :)
future/followup: should this be done in `vim_getenv` itself?
I don't think we need this for `CreateProcessW` arg0. We should try dropping this in a followup.
also here? no, because `os_exepath` already took care of it.
another case for `vim_getenv`
`expand_env` should do this?
Eh, if we can add a param, that would work—`f_getenv`/`eval` also use it to retrieve variables that aren’t path-related.

`argv0` actually comes from `main.c`, where it's already normalized. I only added `TO_SLASH(buf)` above because `PATH` can introduce backslashes. Another way would be to replace `os_getenv("PATH")` with `vim_getenv("PATH", true)`. :D
`\` in `expand_env` could also be used for escaping (before expanding to an actual path), so I cautiously normalized only the env parts.

I figured it out. During its `Init`, `cmd.exe` calls `CheckSwitches` to parse the command line, which splits `C:/WINDOWS/system32/cmd.exe` into sth like `... /c md.exe`. `/c` means **run the follow command and exit** ,  and `md.exe` means `mkdir .exe`. 😅
Thanks for confirming. Yes, anything that treats `/` as a switch will be confused.

comment_regular_data.body:
This looks like a good first step. Ideally shouldn't do too much more than this, assuming CI can be fixed :)

Will plan to merge this quickly after 0.12 release, which is planned for ~1 month from now.

may touch more test files, but changes under `src` shouldn't grow much. Actually, I have tried to reduce the scope in earlier revisions, but it still touches 20+ files, maybe we can see if this can be broken down into smaller PRs after I finishing updateing the tests. :D
That's fine, no need to contort this into weird shapes. Just wanted to set approximate expectations :)
Many of the test changes defeat the entire point of these tests.

> Many of the test changes defeat the entire point of these tests.



Which tests specifically? I don't see much reason to display options or buffer names with platform-specific slashes, so e.g. the changes `test/functional/options/defaults_spec.lua` look ok.

But some things like `fnamemodify()` may be required to return `\` slashes, if they are used by plugins for "normalization". And 'shellslash' is an open question, but might be something we can temporarily break and see what the blast radius is.

> Which tests specifically? 

Mostly the 'shellslash' tests. What even is the point of 'shellslash' if it isn't respected by `expand()` etc.?

> I don't see much reason to display options or buffer names with platform-specific slashes

What do you mean by "display"?

> What do you mean by "display"?

- For options: the actual value (and everywhere it is displayed, e.g. `:set`).
- For buffers: in statusline, and anywhere else where mixed slashes was already possible. 
    - Example: if it's possible for `bufname()` to return mixed slashes, then it's safe for us to always return `/` slashes because there was never a guarantee of only `\` slashes.

Tracked in: 
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39382

> > What do you mean by "display"?
> 
>   * For options: the actual value (and everywhere it is displayed, e.g. `:set`).
>
>   * For buffers: in statusline, and anywhere else where mixed slashes was already possible.
>       
>     * Example: if it's possible for `bufname()` to return mixed slashes, then it's safe for us to always return `/` slashes because there was never a guarantee of only `\` slashes.

Yes, I think this makes sense. I was talking about the return value of `expand()` etc..
please try to keep this rebased so we can followup after release :)
will rebase now. I thought you’d be prioritizing some bug fixes after 0.12 release, so I didn’t comment here. I've just been quietly preparing a second PR :D
Again, some of the tests look very confusing after the test modifications done in this PR.

> Again, some of the tests look very confusing after the test modifications done in this PR.

~~Ah, will look into it and see if I can improve anything.~~ Updated :)

BTW, will squash the commits before merging
Not sure why the failing task shows it was interrupted by user. Could it be caused by my force push? 😅

> Some of the `TO_SLASH` in-place replacements should be carefully looked at

Yeah, it's still possible I missed a few, although I went through almost all the related call paths and made changes accordingly (my notes alone run into thousands of lines). BTW, I marked some of the review comments as resolved while replying to them. If that messes with the review flow, I'll avoid doing that next time. 😅

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: fix(path): normalize separator to slash on Windows
